attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the architecture notes, then evaluate the current state of the code.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.
5	
6	## After cycle 2
7	- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
8	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base × first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000…01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
9	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
13	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers — speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
14	- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table — zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again — still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.
18

content.text:
Now let me evaluate the current state. 5 tests are passing. The Green phase just added the cursed multiplier.

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation**

- `runScenario` — matches the contract.
- `item` — now legitimately carries `.cursed`; the cycle-3 rename pays off.
- `cursedMult` — slight abbreviation; could be `cursedMultiplier`.
- `itemBase` — refers to "base price for this item after item-level modifiers (currently just cursed)." Name is OK but slightly loaded.

**Step 2: Current APP Mass**

```
function runScenario body (lines 17–22):
- const { steps } = scenario as Scenario;   // 1 binding (steps), 1 invocation (cast), 1 binding (scenario)
- const item = steps[0].items[0];           // 1 binding, 2 constants (0, 0), 2 invocations (index)
- const cursedMult = item.cursed ? CURSED_MULTIPLIER : 1;  // 1 binding, 1 conditional, 1 constant (1)
- const itemBase = BASE_PRICE[item.type] * cursedMult;     // 1 binding, 2 invocations (index, *)
- const premium = Math.round(itemBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE;  // 1 binding, 3 invocations (round, *, +)
- return { results: [{ premium }] };        // 0 new

Approximate count:
- Constants: ~3 (0, 0, 1) = 3
- Bindings: ~7 (scenario, steps, item, cursedMult, itemBase, premium, plus refs) = 7+
- Invocations: ~10 = 20
- Conditionals: 1 = 4
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 = 0

Total: ~34
```

**Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules**

- **Rule 1 (Tests pass)**: 5 passing.
- **Rule 2 (Reveal intent)**: The body now reads as a 3-step pipeline: base price → apply item modifiers (cursed) → apply customer loading + admin fee. That progression IS the domain. Good.
- **Rule 3 (No duplication)**: None present yet. But upcoming `enchantment` test (cycle 7) will add another item-level multiplier — same shape as `cursed`. The right move is to wait for that duplication driver before extracting, per APP and per the cycle-3 note.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest elements)**: `itemBase` and `cursedMult` are explaining variables. Could inline into one expression, but that would crush the pipeline structure (Rule 2 > APP).

**Naming refinement**: `cursedMult` is the only minor smell. Spelling it `cursedMultiplier` matches the module constant naming (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`) and removes the abbreviation. Tiny but pays off when `enchantmentMultiplier` joins it next cycle — consistent naming will pattern-match cleanly.

I'll make this one minimal naming improvement.

input.old_string:
const cursedMult = item.cursed ? CURSED_MULTIPLIER : 1;
  const itemBase = BASE_PRICE[item.type] * cursedMult;
input.new_string:
const cursedMultiplier = item.cursed ? CURSED_MULTIPLIER : 1;
  const itemBase = BASE_PRICE[item.type] * cursedMultiplier;

No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). input.new_string:
## After cycle 4
- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table — zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again — still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.

## After cycle 5
- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` → `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee).
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` — having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete

**Naming Evaluation (first priority)**: `runScenario` still matches the contract; `item` carries `.cursed` correctly now (cycle-3 rename validated). The only smell was the local `cursedMult` — an abbreviation that broke pattern with the fully-spelled module constants (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`).

**Refactoring applied**:
- Renamed local binding `cursedMult` → `cursedMultiplier` (Rule 2: Reveal Intent).
- Why this one and not extraction: only one item modifier exists right now, so a `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` helper would be speculative (no Rule 3 duplication driver). The next cycle adds `enchantment` — that's when the extraction will be forced and the consistent `*Multiplier` naming will pay off.

**Mass Change**: ~34 → ~34 (binding name length doesn't affect APP count; the refactoring is pure naming).

**Tests**: 5 passed, 19 todo. All green.

**Implementation now reads as**:
```
const cursedMultiplier = item.cursed ? CURSED_MULTIPLIER : 1;
const itemBase = BASE_PRICE[item.type] * cursedMultiplier;
const premium = Math.round(itemBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE;
```
The three-line pipeline (base lookup → item modifiers → customer loading + fee) makes the upcoming `enchantment` insertion site obvious.

Proceeding to the next test (Test 6: sword with enchantment 5 → 148).
